figures.gloss: The Ituri forest itself, addressed as a person: mother, father, friend, even lover. It gives food, shelter, and affection to its children; when misfortune comes the forest is not angry but asleep — and must be woken and made glad with song.
figures.facet: Theism with no throne: the Mbuti god is the total environment, benevolent by default — and it exceeds the mirror's mother-archetype by being deliberately both parents at once, a deity defined by intimacy rather than power.
figures.provenance: Colin Turnbull, The Forest People (1961) and Wayward Servants (1965). Turnbull's portrait is beloved and contested — Grinker (Houses in the Rainforest, 1994) and later Ituri ethnographers fault his romantic isolation of the Mbuti from their village neighbours — but the forest-as-parent address is direct informant testimony, whatever frame is put around it.

figures.gloss: After a death or in deep crisis the men bring out the molimo — a long trumpet kept hidden in the trees, in Turnbull's day sometimes a plain metal drainpipe — and for nights on end it answers the men's fireside songs from the darkness, animal-voiced, until the forest is awake and glad again.
figures.facet: The famous drainpipe is the theology: what sanctifies is not the object but the singing — a religion whose central 'ritual instrument' can be junk metal because the sacrament is the sound and the sitting together.
figures.provenance: Turnbull, The Forest People (1961), the classic account of the great molimo at Epulu; reported earlier, and framed differently, in Schebesta's 1920s-30s Ituri work.

figures.gloss: Religion in the present tense: no ancestor cult, no priests, no stored obligations to gods or the dead — the forest gives today as it gave yesterday, and ritual's job is to keep the relationship glad, not to bank credit against a hereafter.
figures.facet: Woodburn's immediate-return category made theological: societies that store nothing store no eschatology either — the Mbuti case is the cleanest demonstration that a religion can run almost entirely without deferral.
figures.provenance: James Woodburn, 'Egalitarian Societies' (1982), which classes the Mbuti — with the Ju|'hoansi San and Hadza — as immediate-return; Turnbull's ethnography supplies the religious content.

figures.gloss: When a girl first menstruates the camp celebrates: she and her age-mates enter the elima house to learn the songs of adulthood, singing challenges to the young men outside — first blood greeted as a gift and a joy, not a danger to be secluded.
figures.facet: A precise inversion of the region's menarche-fear: the same biological moment that the neighbouring farming villages treat as polluting, the forest camp sings as a blessing — one datum, two opposite ritual signs.
figures.provenance: Turnbull, The Forest People (1961) and Wayward Servants (1965); the villager-contrast is Turnbull's own framing and shares in the critiques of his forest/village dichotomy.

figures.gloss: The name older ethnography gives the forest-god of the Ituri: keeper of the game, lord of the dead, owner of fire in some tellings. Schebesta built him into a pygmy high god; Turnbull's informants mostly folded the name back into the forest itself.
figures.facet: A figure whose chief lesson is methodological: the missionary-ethnographer found a Supreme Being where the romantic ethnographer found none — Tore is the test-strip showing how much of a 'god' can be an artifact of who is asking.
figures.provenance: Paul Schebesta's Ituri fieldwork (1920s-30s); Turnbull, Wayward Servants (1965), which directly disputes Schebesta's theistic framing.

edges.note: The rite exists for the parent: the molimo's whole purpose is to wake the sleeping forest and delight it with song when its children are in grief.

GOLD SEAM: Woodburn's immediate-return category holds both peoples — religions whose worship is an act performed now (the dance, the song) rather than a debt carried forward; the shared category is scholarly, the traditions unrelated.
Two all-night, sound-driven repairs of the world — the trance circle heals through the body, the molimo through the woken forest; forager religions whose crisis-response is to sing until dawn.
Two African forager gods dissolved into the environment — Kaang recedes into the game and the hunt, the Mbuti god simply is the forest; divinity as habitat rather than throne, reached independently.

Menarche as cosmic potency in both — with opposite signs: the |Xam girl's first blood endangers the band unless hedged with !Khwa's taboos, while the Mbuti girl's is sung as a gift; one moment, two valences.
The concealed sound-instrument as the sacred's voice — the molimo trumpet and the bora bullroarer, both officially kept from the uninitiated, both making the invisible audible at the camp's edge.

dossier.essence: The religion of the Mbuti and neighbouring forest-forager peoples of the Ituri rainforest in the DR Congo — a small-scale, priestless hunter-gatherer tradition known to the outside world chiefly through Schebesta's and Turnbull's 20th-century ethnographies. Its central conviction is that the forest itself is a benevolent parent — mother and father at once — who gives its children food, shelter, and affection.
dossier.cosmos: The forest (ndura) is the total sacred environment: when misfortune strikes, the forest is not angry but asleep, and must be woken and gladdened with song. Older ethnography names a forest god, Tore, keeper of the game — a figure Schebesta systematized as a high god and Turnbull's informants largely dissolved back into the forest itself. There is no ancestor cult and little eschatology: religion runs almost wholly in the present tense, with no stored debts to gods or the dead.
dossier.practice: In deep crisis or after a death the men bring out the molimo trumpet — kept hidden in the trees, in Turnbull's day sometimes a plain metal pipe — and for nights on end the camp sings to it and it answers, animal-voiced, until the forest is awake again. A girl's first menstruation is celebrated rather than feared in the elima festival, where she and her age-mates learn the songs of adulthood. There are no priests, temples, or full-time specialists; song and shared presence are the sacrament.
dossier.canon: No writing — an oral corpus of songs and legends; the classic records are Schebesta's Ituri ethnography (1920s-30s) and Turnbull's 'The Forest People' (1961) and 'Wayward Servants' (1965).
dossier.reflects: Religion as immediate relationship with a living environment — divinity located in habitat rather than heaven, tended by song instead of sacrifice, creed, or stored obligation.
sources: Colin M. Turnbull, 'Wayward Servants: The Two Worlds of the African Pygmies' (1965)

links.cite: Colin M. Turnbull, 'Wayward Servants: The Two Worlds of the African Pygmies' (1965)
